1: The speaker poses a series of questions asking when his fortune changed for the worse. While the opening four words are easily understood, the relative clause is less straightforward, perhaps reflecting the speaker’s distress or confusion. The subject and verb of the relative clause are postponed to the end of the following verse, and adjectives are twice separated from the nouns they modify. amanti: a substantive adjective from the participle of amō = “to the one loving.” The possessive adjective in v. 3 indicates that the “one loving” is the speaker.
2: non albae … aues: “not-white birds” are implicitly “dark” and therefore ominous. Ovid elsewhere construes birds associated with shadows and the night (e.g., owls in Amores 1.12) as representing bad omens.
3: quodue: quod is an interrogative adjective separated from sidus, the word it modifies. -ue is enclitic, meaning it “leans on” the end of another word. As a conjunction, it carries the sense of “or” and can be rendered first in the clause. putem: an independent deliberative subjunctive that introduces indirect statement. occurrere: a compound verb whose object is in the dative.
4: The syntax of this verse resembles that of the previous one, although the deliberative subjunctive verb that introduces indirect statement appears at the end of the sentence.
5: The speaker reveals how his fortune has changed: the exclusivity of his relationship with the puella (revealed in verse 16 to be Corinna) seems to be in jeopardy. quae: supplying a feminine singular antecedent like ea makes rendering this and the following relative clause in English easier. dicta mea est = “was said to be mine.” A form of esse is implied.
6: uereor: introduces a fear clause, with ne indicating a fear something may happen (and an accompanying hope that it won’t). sit habenda: a passive periphrastic or “gerundive of obligation” with a dative of agent mihi. The verb habeō connotes possession that could apply equally to a person (e.g., an enslaved individual) or an object.
7: The speaker realizes his own writings may have played a role in the woman’s popularity. an: an interrogative participle that introduces a second question which suggests an alternative answer to the first (OLD s.v. an 3).
8: sic erit: the second question from verse 7 is answered in the affirmative. prostitit: the woman is depicted as a prostitute, and the speaker’s talent is the implicit cause.
10: uendibilis: a predicate nominative after facta … est. Scansion shows culpa to be ablative. Because of elision, the quantity of the final -a in mea cannot be known, but it would be odd for culpa to be unmodified, especially when the speaker is stressing his own responsibility. On the other hand, the irony of here claiming the puella as his own is strong.
11: This couplet shows the speaker realizing that his own actions, perhaps even as depicted in specific poems of the Amores, have outlined various avenues to approach his puella. The revelation is comparable to a psychological thriller (think the 1999 film Fight Club; vague spoiler to follow), in which the narrator realizes that he has done the things he attributed to others—that he has become one of the enemies he fought against (Amores 2.12.3-4). In this couplet, the speaker recognizes himself in the procuress (lena) named Dipsas, who coaches the puella in Amores 1.8; in the husband (maritus) who grants his rival easy access in Amores 2.19; in the guard (ianitor) whom the speaker tries unsuccessfully to persuade to grant him access in Amores 1.6 or the attendant (custos) named Bagoas in Amores 2.2, a eunuch to whom the speaker outlines all the ways he can and should shirk his duties. me lenone … duce me: ablative absolutes with implied forms of the present active participle of esse (nonexistent in Classical Latin). perductus: a form of esse is implied. The verb can represent the “handing over” or “inducing” done by a pimp or procurer (OLD s.v. perducō 1e).
12: The speaker presents himself as the custos/ianitor of the woman’s house (cf. Amores 3.8.63-64).
13: dubium: a form of esse is implied; the clause dubium est introduces an indirect question continued by the interrogative participle an (see remarks on verse 7, above). The subject of prosint appears in the latter half of the verse. certe: whatever his doubts about the benefits of poetry, the speaker is confident of the detriment it has done. #IIIF Kenney suggests that Manuscript P reads semper for certe, but the entire line is now obscured.
14: illa: this antecedent’s pronoun is carmina from verse 13 fuere: a syncopated form of fuerunt. inuidiae nostris … bonis: a double dative construction with inuidiae as a dative of purpose and nostris bonis as a dative of reference: “they have been a source of envy for our goods.”
15: The speaker identifies poetic paths not taken. cum: the three circumstantial clauses in this verse all use foret. sola in verse 16 confirms that all three occasions of cum have the adversative force of “although.” Thebae: a proper noun in the plural indicates the Greek polis, or city-state, of Thebes, the most famous king of which was Oedipus. (Compare how Athens—Athenae—is also plural in Latin and English.) The name here likely represents the material of tragedy, although it may also refer to epic. Statius, a Roman poet of the 1st c. CE, wrote an epic centering on Theban events after Oedipus’s self-blinding. Troia: Troy. The name here likely represents the material of Homeric epic. Caesaris acta: This designation likely represents the material of recent history, although there is nothing to prohibit these deeds from being celebrated in verse.
16: Corinna: the woman’s identities as love object and poetic subject matter are collapsed into each other. Cf. the remarks on Delia and Nemesis, Tibullus’s named love interests, in 3.9.31-32.
17: In this and the next verse, the speaker expresses a desire to have been less successful in his poetic undertakings. utinam tetigissem: an optative subjunctive expressing an unattainable wish for the past. auersis … Musis: the distance between the adjective and noun in this ablative absolute visually represents the separation the speaker wishes to have attained from the Muses’ inspiration.
18: Phoebus: Apollo, in his role as patron of poetry and music. et: the postponed conjunction can be translated before Phoebus. destituisset: another optative subjunctive continuing the wish from the previous verse.
19: The Latin word order is challenging. Rendering the couplet in English word order might result in nec tamen est mos audire poetas ut testes. ut testes = “as (legal) witnesses” audire: translated as a complement to nec … mos est = “…nor is it customary to listen…” The speaker continues to use legal language in which on “hears” or “listens to” witnesses (OLD s.v. audire 7b). Simultaneously, poetry is presented as an oral medium.
20: malueram: the pluperfect indicative of this verb can also indicate an unattained wish (“I had preferred” = “I would have preferred”).
21: per nos: the speaker catalogues the fantastic things reported “by us poets.” Scylla: two figures named Scylla are here conflated. One is the sea monster best known from the Odyssey where she is paired with a whirlpool known as Charybdis. In the Metamorphoses, Ovid depicts her as having vicious dogs instead of legs (Met. 14.1-74). The other is the daughter of Nisus, a king of Megara whose purple hair rendered him and his city invulnerable. This princess Scylla fell in love with the Cretan king Minos when he was besieging Megara and so betrayed her father by cutting off a lock of his purple hair and presenting it to her love interest. In the Metamorphoses, Ovid portrays this Scylla as being transformed into a bird (Met. 8.1-151), but the speaker here suggests that the princess and sea monster are one and the same. patri: dative of disadvantage indicating the victim of Scylla’s theft.
22: premit: the verb matter-of-factly expresses the point of contact between the woman and her monstrous parts. She “presses” the dogs with her groin. pube … inguinibus: the ablatives convey with what parts of her body the dogs are in contact. The double emphasis on this Scylla’s groin may be meant to critique the amatory motivation of her betrayal.
24: Abantiades: a patronymic indicating a descendent of Abas, the great-grandfather of Perseus. The name explains the references to winged sandals and Medusan hair in the previous verse and anticipates the reference to Pegasus at the end of this one.
25: idem: the adjective emphasizes the plural subject of porreximus: “We same ones stretched…” Tityon: the accusative of Tityus in Greek. Tityus, the son of Zeus, was a giant who attempted to rape Leto, the mother of Artemis and Apollo. His eternal punishment was to have his huge body stretched out and chained over nine acres in Tartarus as vultures ate his regenerating liver.
26: fecimus: poetic creation continues to be emphasized. cani: a reference to Cerberus’s three heads (and mouths)
27: Enceladon: the accusative of Enceladus in Greek. Enceladus, another giant, here with a thousand shoulders and so bearing some resemblance to the hundred-handed Briareus. iaculantem: intransitive (OLD s.v. iaculor 1a). The projectiles are implied in the verb.
28: uiros: another object of fecimus in verse 27. The lack of a conjunction (asyndeton) contributes a sense of length to the list. ambiguae … uirginis: the hybrid maiden describes a single Siren. One depiction of the Sirens on a red-figure vase portrays them with the heads of women but the bodies of large birds. https://www.britishmuseum.org/collection/object/G_1843-1103-31
29: Aeolios: an adjective describing anything connected with Aeolus, the god of the winds who offers aid to Odysseus in Odyssey Book 10 by enclosing in a wine sack all the winds but the West Wind, which will propel his ships home. Ithacis: the island of Ithaca was home to Odysseus and his men. inclusimus: the structure of the line, with the verb surrounded by pairs of adjective and nouns, emphasizes the sense of the main verb. Euros: a Greek spelling of Eurus, the East Wind. The speaker implies that wineskins did not contain all the winds but just the East Wind.
30: proditor…Tantalus: the betrayal of Tantalus could be his sharing of information he wasn’t supposed to or his gift of ambrosial to mortals. See further remarks on Amores 7.51, where he is referred to as uulgator. Note that this verse does not foreground the work of the poets. The speaker simply places Tantalus in the scene, echoing the graphic word order of the previous verse.
31: Niobe: ablative of Niobe, daughter of Tantalus and a mother who boasted of her many children and compared herself to Leto, mother of Apollo and Artemis. As punishment, her children were slain by Apollo and Artemis. She turned to stone while mourning. de uirgine…ursam: a reference to Callisto, a nymph who followed Diana but was impregnated by Jupiter, expelled from Diana’s retinue, and punished by Juno by being turned into a bear. Again, the actions of gods are claimed as poets’ own.
32: Odrysium: accusative of Odrysius. The Odrysian region here refers to Thrace and invokes the tale of Tereus, Procne, Philomela, and Itys, recounted at length in Ovid’s Metamorphoses 6.401-674. The Thracian (Odrysian) king Tereus marries Procne, princess of Athens, and returns to Thrace, but some years later he abducts and rapes her sister Philomela when she comes to visit Procne. He then removes Philomela’s tongue so she can tell no one of his crimes. When, unbeknownst to Tereus, Philomela and Procne are eventually reunited and the extent of Tereus’ crimes are understood, Procne decides to punish her husband by feeding their young son Itys to him. Before Tereus can retaliate, Philomela is transformed into a swallow, Procne a nightingale, and Tereus a hoopoe bird. Cecropis: genitive of Cecrops. Cecrops was the mythical founder of Athens. His name stands in for Athens itself, the home to both Procne and Philomela. Thus, “the bird of Cecrops” could refer to either sister, although the reference to singing makes the nightingale (Procne) more apt, as if the transformed mother calls for the son she murdered. Ityn: the accusative of Itys in Greek.
33: aues: Multiple myths involve the transformation of Jupiter into a bird as he pursues amorous interests. At Amores 1.3.22, the speaker refers to Jupiter’s affair with Leda when disguised as a swan. At Metamorphoses 10.155-161, Orpheus sings of Zeus’s transformation into an eagle and subsequent abduction of Ganymede. aurum: a reference to the tale of Jupiter and Danae. See the remarks on Amores 3.8.29 for an overview of the myth.
34: taurus: Jupiter, disguised as a bull, abducted the Phoenician princess Europa and swam to Crete with her on his back.
35: Protea: an accusative form of Proteus in Greek. The god Proteus is known both for his shape-shifting abilities and his prophetic knowledge. In Odyssey 4.455-8, he rapidly shifts through various animate and inanimate forms. The English adjective “protean” derives from his name. referam: most comfortably translated as a independent subjunctive (potential): “why would I recount…” Thebanaque semina, dentes: the founding of Thebes by Cadmus entailed his slaying of a dragon and the sowing of the dragon’s teeth from which a race of warriors arose. These earth-born warriors immediately fought one another, the survivors becoming the first inhabitants of Thebes. Ovid offers a version of this myth in Metamorphoses 3.1-137.
36: The speaker alludes to another tale of sown dragon teeth. When Jason and the Argonauts traveled to Colchis to retrieve the Golden Fleece, the young hero, with the help of Medea’s advice and magic, successfully yoked two fire breathing bulls, plowed a field with them, and sowed the field with dragon teeth. In the version of the tale recounted in the Argonautica by the Greek poet Apollonius of Rhodes (3rd c. BCE), the teeth are those of the same dragon that Cadmus slayed. qui: the antecedent appears at the end of the verse. uomerent: subjunctive in a relative clause embedded in indirect statement. fuisse: an infinitive in indirect statement, introduced by referam in verse 35. The indirect statement continues in the following couplet.
37: flere: an infinitive in indirect statement, with an accusative subject of tuas…sorores. The infinitive functions transitively (e.g., “…to cry tears.”) electra: accusative plural neuter auriga: the speaker addresses a charioteer in the vocative. The reference to sisters who weep amber indicates that the charioteer is Phaethon. In Metamorphoses 2.31-366, Phaethon’s father Sol (the sun) permits the youth to pilot his chariot, but the young charioteer loses control, tearing up the earth in the process, and is struck out of the air by Jupiter before he can do further damage to the world. His mourning sisters become poplar trees, and their tears become amber.
38: quaeque: the enclitic conjunction que is attached to a relative pronoun to indicate a new item in the list. Supplying rates as an antecedent for quae (i.e., moving it out of the relative clause) may ease the translation of this verse, which is a continuation of the indirect statement introduced by referam: quid referam rates quae fuerint…. rates: a reference to the transformation of Aeneas’s ships into nymphs (Aeneid 9.77-122). Vergil’s Aeneid recounts the travels of the Trojan hero Aeneas from Troy to the site that will become Rome, where he wars with indigenous Italians. When the Italian general Turnus attempts to set fire to the Trojan fleet, which was constructed of sacred wood from Mt. Ida, the ships transform into sea nymphs and escape Turnus’s destruction. fuerint: subjunctive in a relative clause in indirect statement. The tense stresses that the boats are no longer boats. (A&G 585).
39: Supplying an infinitive form of esse permits us to render both verses in this couplet as continuations of the indirect statement (e.g., auersum esse and secuta esse) first introduced by referam. Alternatively, the participles and nouns can be translated as simple direct objects (like Protea in verse 35). mensis furialibus Atrei: another tale of vengeful child cannibalism (see the remarks on verse 32). The version of the tale best known from antiquity, composed as the play Thyestes in the 1st c. AD by Seneca the Younger, postdates Ovid. Atreus and his brother Thyestes had long battled for control of Argos, and even when Atreus has secured the throne, he still desires to exact vengeance on his brother for past wrongs. He invites Thyestes under the guise of a détente, then murders Thyestes’ sons, and feeds them to their unwitting father, precipitating such ominous celestial effects as an eclipse. Atrei: genitive of Atreus
40: saxa secuta lyram: a reference to Amphion who, with his brother Zethus, constructed the stone walls surrounding Thebes. While Zethus toiled physically to build the wall, Amphion was said to have compelled large rocks to fall into place with his music alone.
42: obligat: retain the subject from the previous verse. historica: scansion shows this to be ablative and to modify fide. nec: this connects the present verse to the preceding one and should likely be rendered first in English translation. sua uerba = “its own words” (i.e., the products of “poetic license”)
43: Note the enjambment of this and the following verse, thus adding emphasis to the subject femina. et: a translation of “also” better conveys the logical correspondence between this and the previous couplet. debuerat…uideri = “ought to have seemed.” English does not use a past tense of “ought.” falso: an adverb meaning “falsely” laudata: an implied esse makes this a complementary infinitive to uideri (itself a complement to debuerat).
44: mihi: dative as the object of nocet. uestra: the speaker addresses a plural audience, perhaps to be understood as the readership itself.
